title: Calisthenics A
subtitle: Upper Body & Skills
coaching_note: L-sit is the featured skill goal for this session, trained alongside calisthenics_b to build hold time through frequency. Wall holds and peel-offs keep handstand progressing as a secondary skill without competing for focus. Push work goes after skill work, not before, so the nervous system is fresh for balance and control. Dips are band-assisted to start - a beginner-appropriate load that lets you build full range of motion before adding bodyweight-only reps. Wrist and forearm conditioning at the end supports every parallette and handstand exercise in this session, so don't skip it.

exercises.name: Wrist circles & stretches
exercises.form_cue: Full range of motion, both directions. Spend extra time on any stiff spots.
exercises.why: Wrists take load in every handstand and parallette exercise - non-negotiable warm-up

exercises.name: Band overhead pass-throughs
exercises.form_cue: Arms straight, wide grip. Move slow through the full arc - chest to overhead to behind.
exercises.why: Opens shoulder flexion range needed for overhead handstand position

exercises.name: Shoulder circles (arm swings)
exercises.why: Gets blood into the shoulder joint before loading

exercises.name: Cat-cow
exercises.form_cue: Slow and deliberate. Full spinal flexion and extension.
exercises.why: Wakes up the spine and core - foundation for hollow body position

exercises.name: Wall walks
exercises.form_cue: Hands stay shoulder-width. Walk feet up the wall until arms are vertical. Squeeze core and glutes at the top. Walk back down with control.
exercises.why: Builds the shoulder strength and body alignment needed for a freestanding handstand

exercises.name: Chest-to-wall handstand holds
exercises.form_cue: Face the wall, hands ~15cm from the base. Kick up to hold. Arms locked, core tight, push the floor away. Find the balance point.
exercises.why: Chest-to-wall puts you in the correct stacked position - trains the balance feel without the fear of falling forward

exercises.name: Peel-off attempts
exercises.form_cue: From chest-to-wall hold, slowly peel feet off one at a time. Find the balance point. Don't chase it - let it come.
exercises.why: Transition to freestanding - attempt only when wall holds feel solid

exercises.name: Tuck L-sit holds (parallettes)
exercises.form_cue: Hands on parallettes. Arms locked straight. Lift hips off the ground, bring knees to chest. Hold. Don't let elbows bend.
exercises.why: Foundation of the L-sit skill. Build hold time before extending legs. Training this twice a week (paired with calisthenics_b) beats one big weekly dose for skill work.

exercises.name: One-leg L-sit attempts
exercises.form_cue: From tuck hold, extend one leg straight. Other leg stays tucked. Try to hold. This is the bridge to a full L-sit.
exercises.why: Progression toward full L-sit - attempt when tuck hold is consistent at 10s

exercises.name: Parallette push-ups
exercises.form_cue: Full depth - chest below the bars. Straight body, squeeze core. Slow down (2s), explode up.
exercises.why: Greater depth than floor push-ups builds more chest and tricep range. Dropped to 2 sets - beginner volume.

exercises.name: Elevated pike push-ups
exercises.form_cue: Feet on a chair or box, hips high, head toward the floor. Elbows track back, not out. This is a shoulder press, not a push-up.
exercises.why: Directly trains the overhead pressing strength needed for handstand push-ups. Dropped to 2 sets - beginner volume.

exercises.name: Band-assisted parallette dips
exercises.form_cue: Loop the resistance band under the parallettes and over your shoulders/knees to assist the press. Full ROM - lower until upper arms are parallel to the ground, full lockout at top. Slow eccentric (2-3s down). If you don't have the band set up, sub bench dips instead. Stop if you feel any joint pain, not just muscle fatigue.
exercises.why: Full-ROM parallette dips load the elbow and shoulder hard - too much for a beginner all at once. Band assistance (or bench dips) builds the tricep/chest work without the load spike.

exercises.name: Hollow body holds
exercises.form_cue: Lower back pressed flat. Arms overhead, legs straight and low. If back arches, bring legs higher. This shape is the body position in a handstand.
exercises.why: The hollow body is non-negotiable for a straight handstand - train it every session. Building toward 60s - add time only once 40s holds the shape clean.

exercises.name: Plank
exercises.form_cue: Straight line from head to heel. Don't let hips sag or pike. Push the floor away.

exercises.name: Eccentric wrist curls
exercises.form_cue: Use other hand to curl up, then slowly lower with the working arm over 3-4 seconds. Light weight only.
exercises.why: Eccentric load builds tendon resilience in the wrist flexors - good maintenance for anyone putting regular load through their wrists in handstand and parallette work

exercises.name: Reverse wrist curls
exercises.form_cue: Palm down. Curl the wrist up, lower slowly. Light weight.
exercises.why: Strengthens the extensor side so flexor and extensor strength stay balanced

exercises.name: Wrist flexor stretch
exercises.form_cue: Arm straight, palm facing up, gently pull fingers back with other hand. Hold, don't bounce.
exercises.why: Lengthens the flexor tendons under tension - pairs with the eccentric curls for full forearm health

exercises.name: Chest opener stretch
exercises.form_cue: Arms behind back, clasp hands, squeeze shoulder blades, open chest toward ceiling.
exercises.why: Counterbalances the pressing work - keeps posture from rounding forward

exercises.name: Foam roll: lats and upper back
exercises.form_cue: Slow passes, pause 2-3 seconds on tight spots. Work lats on each side, then thoracic spine.
exercises.why: Releases the shoulder and back tension from wall walks and pushing work

progression_notes: Handstand: add 5s to wall holds each week. Peel-offs: attempt when holds feel easy. L-sit: add 2s to tuck holds each week, attempt one-leg extension when 10s is consistent - keep this block in sync with calisthenics_b's progression. Push work: return to 3 sets once 2x sets feels below RPE 6. Band-assisted dips: reduce band assistance, then move to bodyweight bench/parallette dips once reps feel solid and any joints stay quiet. Pike push-ups: elevate feet higher as shoulders get stronger. Hollow body: add 5s per week toward a 60s target - don't add time if the lower back arches.
